stories.title: The Coatrack
stories.content:
The coatrack sat quietly in the corner of the entry way. Tucked away it, went unnoticed for most seasons of the year. Only during winters would it bear a heavier load. When spring rolled around, it'd shed its weight, like a tree dropping its leaves. Empty, it'd wait patiently for the next rainstorm or cold front.

When the front door opened it'd look for what coats were being work, not by whom they were worn. Parties were especially exciting, but they happened rarely. It had only ever known one thing - coats.

Then one day,  hat was put on it. The next day, a bike helmet. A new visitor was frequenting the household. Quickly the new visitor became a visitor, then a regular, and then a members of the household. With each day, as they came and went, something new was added to the coat rack's branches. A hula-hoop, a scarf, a tote bag, a keychain, and for a short time, a candy necklace, were some of its favorite items it hung. The coatrack had always been standing storage, but now it was a artful decoration. It changed based on the time off day. And grew gradually week over week. It was becoming something. At the end of each day, for the first time, it felt full. So as the nights rolled in, the rack would sit excitingly in the corner and stretch its arms further making room for tomorrow's addition.

stories.title: Biggest Thumb
stories.content:
His thumb looked bigger. It was a little too long. He put his hands together to compare. It was longer, he was sure of it.

 The next day, Peter measured again. His thumb nearly reached the knuckle of his index finger now. He immediately scheduled an appointment with his doctor.

 "Ive seen this before", his doctor said while pulling and prodding at his thumb.

 "Your thumb wants to be more like your other fingers. Just be glad your other fingers don’t want to be more like your thumb. You’d have quite the stubby hand. What a disaster that’d be".

 How was this not a disaster, Peter panicked. This was a catastrophe. 15 minutes and $350 later, Peter was on his way home no closer to solving his thumbs' sudden reach for new heights. With no other ideas to stop or stunt it, he let it grow.

 Soon enough, Peter found advantages to his thumb’s new length. His grip strength was superb, he was unbeatable at thumb wars, and his pointing never went unnoticed.

Eventually, Peter began to wonder when his other thumb would catch up and grow already. Of all his fingers, especially his other thumb, it felt a little too short.

stories.title: Bed Dough
stories.content:
Some nights I wish I slept on bread dough. It’d be my loaf of  bed. It’s thick round edges would rise from the floor each morning as the son peeked through the window. Slowly it’d grow until it’d meet my night stand. When dusk came, it’d be light and fluffy.

With a slight dome at its center, it’d be ready for me to sink into it. After brushing my teeth and putting on pajamas, I’d take a running start and jump into bed. My body would spring back from its surface and then softly lower into it like a bubble that slowly losing its air without ever popping. It’d fall asleep instantly.

When I’d wake in the morning, I’d listen tot eh low hums and gurgles of the dough rising again. They’d be an alarm I’d never have to set. When I’d finally gain the strength to rise out of bed, I’d sit up and feel the dough stuck to my back pull me back, suggesting it return to my slumber. And some days maybe I’d give in and lay back down spending all day in my load of bed.

stories.title: Girlie Things
stories.content: They were out and about doing girlie things. None of them had ever discussed what a girlie thing was, but when one of them said it, they all nodded in unison. On this sunny spring evening, they found themselves at Rakes’, the only bar in town with a population of 500 and some depending on whether it was tourist season. And for this group, it was the place for girlie things.
